task:
# Relay stage 7: Review
Task: run-all-modes
Working directory: /Users/nicholaswestby/Dev/visual-relay/

## Task input
I’d like to have a drop down to select the mode for the Run All button. There should be a standard option and a “Sequential” option. This one will make sure each task is run in sequence, one at a time, rather than making the first few stages in parallel like they are today.

I might add other modes later on, which is the reason for a drop down instead of a checkbox.

## Manifest
tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.cs
tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.CommandDispatch.cs
tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.StageContracts.cs

## Prior stages
## Stage 1 - Ideate

{
  "summary": "Run all execution modes of the visual-relay system — sequential script, parallel CI matrix, or Nix flake check — to validate mode-specific behaviors end-to-end.",
  "options": [
    "Option A — Sequential shell script with per-mode orchestration",
    "Option B — Parallel matrix run (GitHub Actions matrix or similar CI)",
    "Option C — Nix flake check with mode-specific derivations"
  ]
}

## Stage 2 - Research

{
  "findings": "The visual-relay system exposes at least 16 CLI subcommands (launch/run/build/test/check/format/screenshot/run-task/init/install-hooks/bump-version/inspect/gen-backend-config/guards/provision-mxc), each a distinct execution mode. The relay pipeline itself has 11 stages (Ideate through Commit) with tiered model assignments (cheap/balanced/frontier), varying file-access scopes (none/some/all/driver), and command whitelists. GUI execution offers run-selected, run-all (queue drain), resume, and pause-after-task modes. Platform launchers differ: bash+Nix+brew on macOS/Linux, PowerShell+.NET SDK consent-install on Windows. CI builds in a 3-cell matrix (osx-arm64, osx-x64, win-x64). Sandboxing uses nono (macOS) or mxc (Windows). Tests run either as a full suite (dotnet test, watchdog-protected) or targeted per-file via testFileCmd. The comprehensive check gate runs: source-enum guard → file-size guard (300-line limit) → shell-size guard (20-logic-line limit) → dead-config-field guard → dotnet format verify-no-changes → build → InspectCode → watchdog'd tests (300s timeout) → screenshot render. All backends talk to a local LiteLLM proxy at 127.0.0.1:4000 with 11+ Swival profiles. The Nix flake provisions dev shells for 4 architectures. ~55+ tests cover the codebase across 365+ test files. The current run-all-modes task is in a relay pipeline (stage 2/11) inside a nix-shell working directory /tmp/nix-shell.evmp7T/visual-relay/wt/fbb5c78605a5/plan-20260630062133/run-all-modes, with no .swival/ directory present (filesystem access restricted by the sandbox).",
  "constraints": [
    "Working directory /tmp/nix-shell.evmp7T/visual-relay/wt/fbb5c78605a5/plan-20260630062133/run-all-modes is inside a nix-shell ephemeral environment — no .swival/ directory exists here, so swival-based tool calls fail with filesystem access errors",
    "The visual-relay sandbox (nono on macOS) restricts file writes/deletes to a confined workspace; nono rollback excludes cache/build artifact paths but outside scope will be rolled back",
    "C# source files must stay under 300 lines per file (enforced by FileSizeGuard in check gate)",
    "Shell scripts (bash/PowerShell) must stay under 20 logic lines (enforced by ShellSizeGuard)",
    "All dotnet operations use -m:1 -p:UseSharedCompilation=false (single-node MSBuild, no shared compilation) to avoid hangs in sandboxed/VM environments",
    "Test suite uses a 300s timeout default in check mode, configurable via VISUAL_RELAY_CHECK_TEST_TIMEOUT; hangs are detected via --blame-hang with 20-60s timeouts",
    "No feature branches or PRs — all commits go directly to main with Conventional Commit enforced by commit-msg hook",
    "The GUI control API (HTTP loopback on 127.0.0.1:8765) is the preferred way to drive the running app over the CLI",
    "Queue drain halts at the first non-commit task needing review (writes .relay/DRAIN-HALTED) to prevent cascading failures",
    "Avalonia headless tests must use [AvaloniaFact]/[AvaloniaTheory] — HeadlessUnitTestSession is banned (BannedApiAnalyzers)",
    "Platform-specific: macOS uses nono sandbox + codesign; Windows uses mxc sandbox + different launcher scripts; no official Linux GUI (Nix devShell exists but app targets macOS/Windows releases)",
    "Git and process calls are wrapped behind interfaces (IGitInvoker, ISubagentRunner, IRelayEventSink) for testability",
    "The LiteLLM backend (model proxy) must be reachable at 127.0.0.1:4000 for any real LLM-driven mode",
    "All stages produce artifacts under .relay/<task>/ (ledger, manifest, seals, reports, status entries, run.log)"
  ]
}

## Stage 3 - Diagnose

{
  "evidence": "Stage 1 (Ideate) was configured with files='none' scope, which restricts the swival sandbox to only allow file access within a .swival/ directory. The working directory (a nix-shell ephemeral environment) has no .swival/ subdirectory — confirmed by the stage1 event log at turn 3, where list_files on '.swival/' returned 'path does not exist'. As a result, all 6 tool calls in Stage 1 failed with the error 'outside .swival/ (filesystem access is disabled)', yielding a 0% tool success rate. Stage 1 still completed with outcome='success' because the LLM ideated purely from training knowledge without inspecting any project files — but its output (generic options A/B/C) could not be grounded in the actual visual-relay codebase. In contrast, Stage 2 (Research) had files='some' scope and achieved a 100% tool success rate (62/62 calls), demonstrating that file access works when the scope is broader than 'none'. The root cause: the swival sandbox's builtin mode maps files='none' to 'only .swival/', but no .swival/ directory is provisioned in the nix-shell working directory, leaving the Ideate stage entirely blind to project files.",
  "excerpts": [
    "[stage1 event log, turn 1 tool_result] error: Path '...' resolves to BASEDIR, which is outside .swival/ (filesystem access is disabled)",
    "[stage1 event log, turn 3 tool_result] error: path does not exist: '.../.swival'",
    "[stage1 report, stats] tool_calls_succeeded: 0, tool_calls_failed: 6",
    "[stage1 report, settings] files: 'none'",
    "[stage1 event log, system message] Filesystem access is restricted to .swival/ only. You cannot read or write project files.",
    "[stage2 report, stats] tool_calls_succeeded: 62, tool_calls_failed: 0",
    "[stage2 report, settings] files: 'some'",
    "[RelayStages.cs line 9] Stage(1, 'Ideate', 'cheap', 'none', 'git,ls,cat', ...)",
    "[RelayStages.cs line 10] Stage(2, 'Research', 'cheap', 'some', ...)",
    "[run.log] s3/balanced stage_start name=Diagnose — current stage tier is 'balanced', upgrade from prior 'cheap' stages",
    "[working directory listing] swival.toml exists but no .swival/ directory present"
  ],
  "repro": "1. Enter a nix-shell ephemeral working directory (e.g., /tmp/nix-shell.XXXX/visual-relay/wt/.../plan-.../run-all-modes). 2. Confirm the directory contains swival.toml but no .swival/ subdirectory. 3. Run a relay pipeline task; observe that Stage 1 (Ideate, files='none') has all tool calls fail because the swival builtin sandbox restricts access to .swival/ which doesn't exist. 4. Compare to Stage 2 (Research, files='some') where broader file access is granted and tool calls succeed."
}

## Stage 4 - Plan

{
  "plan": "Create a structural-validation test suite `RunAllModesTests` that exhaustively validates every execution mode in the visual-relay system is properly enumerated, dispatched, and has correct invariants. No runtime integration needed — pure xUnit [Fact]/[Theory] assertions against the static type surface (CommandRouter, RelayStages, CheckCommand, Program.cs switch expressions, flake.nix, release.yml). Three test files, each under the 300-line file-size guard:\n\n**File 1: `tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.cs`** (~250 lines)\n- `AllCliCommands_AreRecognizedByRouter`: Theory with [InlineData] for all 15 CLI commands (launch, run, build, test, format, screenshot, run-task, init, check, inspect, gen-backend-config, guards, install-hooks, bump-version, provision-mxc), asserting CommandRouter.IsKnown returns true. Also asserts the KnownCommands list count equals 15 to catch future drift.\n- `AllGuardSubcommands_AreDispatched`: Validates the 4 guard subcommands (shell-size, file-size, source-enumeration, sync-over-async) exist in the Guards/Program.cs switch expression by inspecting the source text.\n- `AllRelayStages_HaveCorrectMetadata`: Theory enumerating all 11 stages by number, asserting each has correct Name, Tier (cheap/balanced/frontier), Files scope (none/some/all/driver), and non-null SystemPrompt/Contract.\n- `CheckGate_HasAllRequiredSteps`: Reads CheckCommand.cs source and asserts the 9 steps appear in order (source-enumeration, file-size, shell-size, dead-config-field, format --verify-no-changes, build, InspectCode, watchdog'd tests, screenshot).\n- `BackendLifecycle_HasAllCommands`: Asserts the 3 backend commands (start, stop, status) appear in the Backend/Program.cs switch.\n- `NixFlake_HasAllArchitectures`: Reads flake.nix and asserts all 4 arch strings (aarch64-darwin, x86_64-darwin, x86_64-linux, aarch64-linux) appear.\n- `CiMatrix_HasAllReleaseRids`: Reads release.yml and asserts osx-arm64, osx-x64, win-x64 appear in the matrix.\n- `UsageLine_ContainsEverySubcommand`: Asserts CommandRouter.UsageLine contains each known subcommand name.\n\n**File 2: `tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.CommandDispatch.cs`** (~150 lines)\n- `CliProgramDispatch_CoversAllKnownCommands`: Parses Cli/Program.cs source text and asserts the switch expression contains a case arm for every string in CommandRouter.KnownCommands, plus a default/underscore fallback.\n- `GuardProgramDispatch_CoversAllSubcommands`: Parses Guards/Program.cs source text and asserts the switch contains arms for shell-size, file-size, source-enumeration, sync-over-async, and a default.\n- `BackendProgramDispatch_CoversAllCommands`: Parses Backend/Program.cs source text and asserts the switch contains arms for start, stop, status, and a default.\n- `PassthroughCommand_DispatchesRunTask_GenBackendConfig_Guards`: Asserts PassthroughCommand has named methods for run-task, gen-backend-config, and guards (the 3 passthrough subcommands).\n\n**File 3: `tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.StageContracts.cs`** (~200 lines)\n- `AllStages_HaveNonEmptySystemPrompt`: Theory over all 11 stages asserting SystemPrompt is not null or whitespace.\n- `AllStages_HaveNonEmptyContract`: Theory over all 11 stages asserting Contract is not null or whitespace (except stage 11 Commit which has empty contract).\n- `AllStages_HaveValidTier`: Theory over all 11 stages asserting Tier is one of {cheap, balanced, frontier}.\n- `AllStages_HaveValidFilesScope`: Theory over all 11 stages asserting Files is one of {none, some, all, driver}.\n- `AllStages_HaveValidCommands`: Theory over all 11 stages asserting Commands is non-null.\n- `Stages_AreSequential_OneThroughEleven`: Asserts stages are numbered 1-11 without gaps or duplicates.\n- `OnlyCommitStage_HasDriverKind`: Asserts only stage 11 has Kind == \"driver\".\n- `OnlyIdeateStage_HasNoneFilesScope`: Asserts only stage 1 has Files == \"none\".",
  "manifest": [
    "+tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.cs",
    "+tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.CommandDispatch.cs",
    "+tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.StageContracts.cs"
  ]
}

## Stage 5 - Author-tests

{
  "testFiles": [
    "tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.cs",
    "tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.CommandDispatch.cs",
    "tests/VisualRelay.Tests/RunAllModesTests.StageContracts.cs"
  ],
  "rationale": "Structural-validation test suite (35 tests, 112 cases) that exhaustively validates every execution mode in the visual-relay system. File 1 (238 lines): CLI command enumeration (15 commands via IsKnown), guard subcommand dispatch (4 guards via source parse), relay stage metadata (11 stages with Name/Tier/Files), check gate step ordering (9 steps), backend lifecycle commands (3 commands), Nix flake architectures (4 arch strings), CI matrix RIDs (osx-arm64/osx-x64/win-x64), passthrough tool dispatch, and usage line coverage. File 2 (115 lines): switch-expression dispatch coverage — validates Cli/Program.cs has case arms for all 15 known commands plus default, Guards/Program.cs covers 4 subcommands plus default, Backend/Program.cs covers start/stop/status plus default, and PassthroughCommand forwards all 3 tools. File 3 (230 lines): stage contract invariants — non-empty SystemPrompt/OutputContract for LLM stages (empty for driver Commit), valid Tier (cheap/balanced/frontier), valid Files scope (none/some/all/driver), non-empty Commands, sequential 1-11 numbering without duplicates/gaps, Kind exclusivity (only Commit is 'driver'), Files-scope exclusivity (only Ideate+Commit have 'none'), name uniqueness, keyword presence in system prompts, and fenced JSON block in all LLM output contracts. All tests compile and pass against the current codebase (0 failures), validating the existing mode enumeration is complete and correctly structured before the Run All dropdown implementation (stage 6)."
}

## Stage 6 - Implement

{
  "summary": "Implemented Run All mode dropdown with Standard and Sequential options. Created RunAllMode enum (Core/Queue), added SelectedRunAllMode property and RunAllModeOptions list to MainWindowViewModel, added ComboBox to TopBar.axaml binding to those properties, modified RelayQueueController.DrainAsync to accept a RunAllMode parameter that skips the parallel planning phase when Sequential, and threaded SelectedRunAllMode from the ViewModel's DrainQueueAsync to the controller. Added 6 structural-validation tests across the three manifest test files: enum values, dropdown binding, DrainAsync parameter signature, mode threading in Execution.cs, and default/zero-value contracts. All 39 tests pass (0 failures)."
}



End your reply with a single fenced ```json block, nothing after it, matching: { "verdict": "pass"|"changes", "issues": [] }

result.answer:
I have completed my review of the stage 6 diff. Let me consolidate my findings.

## Review Summary

**Implementation verified against the codebase:**

1. **`RunAllMode.cs`** — Clean enum with `Standard` (=0, the zero/default value) and `Sequential`. Doc comments accurate. Tests asserting `default(RunAllMode)==Standard` and `(int)Standard==0` hold.

2. **`RelayQueueController.DrainAsync`** — Signature changed to `(CancellationToken = default, RunAllMode mode = RunAllMode.Standard)`. The `skipPlanning = mode == RunAllMode.Sequential` guard correctly skips Phase 1 (parallel planning) only when Sequential. All other 40+ callers use `DrainAsync()` or `DrainAsync(cts.Token)` — backward compatible (positional CT still maps to first param; named arg used at the one new call site).

3. **Behavioral correctness** — In Standard mode, Phase 1 parallelizes planning stages (1–4) across tasks in worktrees, then Phase 2 runs execution serially. In Sequential mode, Phase 1 is skipped entirely and Phase 2's `while` loop runs each task via `GuiTaskRunner` → `RelayDriver(Resume:true)`, which for a fresh task runs stages 1–11 fully before the next task. This precisely satisfies "each task is run in sequence, one at a time, rather than making the first few stages in parallel."

4. **`MainWindowViewModel.Execution.cs`** — `controller.DrainAsync(mode: SelectedRunAllMode)` correctly threads the selection.

5. **`TopBar.axaml`** — ComboBox added at Column 7, Run All button moved to Column 8, Grid columns expanded 8→9 (indices 0–8, consistent). Binds `ItemsSource`/`SelectedItem`.

6. **Tests** — Verified the structural assertions against actual sources: `RelayStages.cs` (stage 11 = Commit/driver/none/empty prompts; stages 1–10 metadata and keywords all match), `CommandRouter` (exactly 15 known commands), `Program.cs` (`"launch" or "run"` arm + all 15 literals + `_ =>`). All three test files are under the 300-line guard.

**Two minor convention deviations (non-blocking):**
- `RunAllModeOptions` is `static`, bound via plain `{Binding}`. Under compiled bindings (confirmed `AvaloniaUseCompiledBindingsByDefault=true` with `x:DataType` set) this resolves fine; it just deviates from the app's universal instance-property pattern for `ItemsSource` sources.
- `SelectedRunAllMode` is a plain auto-property rather than `[ObservableProperty]`. For a single-consumer TwoWay `SelectedItem` binding this is functionally correct (write-back + one-time initial read work without INPC), but it deviates from the codebase convention.

Neither deviation is a functional defect — the dropdown populates, selection persists, and the mode takes effect.

```json
{ "verdict": "pass", "issues": [] }
```

arguments.command: cd /Users/nicholaswestby/Dev/visual-relay && git status && echo "=== RECENT LOG ===" && git log --oneline -10

arguments.thought: The RepoPaths class isn't found in tests. Let me search for it - the tests reference RepoPaths.Resolve(). Let me find where that's defined, and check the base ViewModel to understand how SelectedRunAllMode should be declared (ObservableProperty vs plain property). The test references SelectedRunAllMode binding which requires INotifyPropertyChanged for Avalonia binding to work. A plain auto-property won't raise change notifications. This is a potential real issue.

Now I understand the ViewModel pattern. All bindable properties in this codebase use `[ObservableProperty]` with a private `_camelCase` field — that's how CommunityToolkit.Mvvm generates the public PascalCase property that raises INotifyPropertyChanged. 

The new `SelectedRunAllMode` property in MainWindowViewModel.Properties.cs is declared as a plain auto-property:
```csharp
public RunAllMode SelectedRunAllMode { get; set; } = RunAllMode.Standard;
```
This does NOT raise PropertyChanged. When bound to a ComboBox's `SelectedItem` in Avalonia, the binding will set the value (TwoWay by default for SelectedItem), so the selection DOES get written back to the property. The issue is: does the ComboBox need PropertyChanged to display the initial value? For SelectedItem, Avalonia reads the initial value on bind, so it should display Standard initially. The bigger question is whether changing it reflects — but since the ComboBox drives it via TwoWay binding, the value gets set. The lack of notifications means other UI won't update, but here nothing else binds to SelectedRunAllMode.

However, there's a subtle concern: Avalonia's x:DataType compiled bindings may warn or the property may not be properly tracked. But functionally, TwoWay SelectedItem binding works without INPC for the write-back direction. The read direction (initial display) also works because Avalonia reads the property value once. So functionally it works, though it deviates from the codebase's `[ObservableProperty]` convention. This is a minor style inconsistency, not a breaking bug.

Let me verify the actual behavior concern more carefully — but first, let me check whether the ViewModelBase is ObservableObject and whether there might be an analyzer that enforces ObservableProperty. Let me also check the RunAllModeOptions being static — binding to a static property works in Avalonia.

Actually the bigger thing to verify: the tests. Let me look at whether the tests actually pass. The stage-6 summary claims "All 39 tests pass (0 failures)". Let me run the targeted test command. But first I need to find the verify command. The task says "use ONLY the targeted test command shown in the ## Verify command section of the prompt" — but I don't see a Verify command section in this prompt. Let me re-read.

The prompt says: "If you need to verify any behavior, use ONLY the targeted test command shown in the ## Verify command section of the prompt." But there's no such section visible. So I should rely on static analysis and not run tests. Let me focus on reviewing the diff.

Let me now carefully assess the implementation against the task requirements and look for real bugs.

Key finding: `AvaloniaUseCompiledBindingsByDefault=true`. With compiled bindings, the TopBar.axaml has `x:DataType="vm:MainWindowViewModel"`. Compiled bindings check at compile time that the property exists on the type — `SelectedRunAllMode` and `RunAllModeOptions` both exist as public members, so compiled bindings will work. The static `RunAllModeOptions` property returns `IReadOnlyList<RunAllMode>`, which is a valid ItemsSource. SelectedItem binding to `SelectedRunAllMode` (a settable property) works in TwoWay.

Now the concern about INPC: With compiled bindings and a plain auto-property (no INPC), the binding still functions for TwoWay writes (ComboBox → property) and one-time reads (property → ComboBox initial). The only thing that breaks is the property → UI update when changed programmatically. Since nothing else observes SelectedRunAllMode, this is acceptable. So no functional bug, but it's an inconsistency with the codebase convention (every other bindable property uses [ObservableProperty]).

Now let me focus on the CORE behavioral question: Does skipping the parallel planning phase actually achieve the user's goal of "each task is run in sequence, one at a time, rather than making the first few stages in parallel"?

Let me understand the Standard mode's parallel planning. In Standard mode, Phase 1 runs the PLANNING stages (1-4) for ALL tasks in parallel (via PlanPhaseRunner.RunPlanPhaseAsync). Then Phase 2 runs the execution stages (5-11) for each task serially. So Standard already runs execution serially; only planning is parallelized across tasks.

In Sequential mode (skipPlanning=true), Phase 1 is skipped entirely. Then Phase 2 runs each task via _runner.RunTaskAsync. But wait — the GuiTaskRunner creates a RelayDriver with `Resume: true`. With Resume:true and no prior planning done, the driver's LoadResumeState finds no prior status.json (fresh task), so firstStageToRun=1, and it runs ALL stages 1-11 for that task serially. Then the next task runs all its stages 1-11. So yes — in Sequential mode, each task runs all its stages one at a time, fully sequential. This matches the user's requirement: "make sure each task is run in sequence, one at a time, rather than making the first few stages in parallel."

So the implementation correctly achieves the goal. Good.

But wait — there's a subtle issue. In Standard mode, Phase 1 planning writes status entries marking stages 1-4 as Done. Then Phase 2's GuiTaskRunner with Resume:true picks up at stage 5. In Sequential mode, Phase 1 is skipped, so NO planning status is written. Phase 2's GuiTaskRunner with Resume:true on a fresh task runs stages 1-11. That's correct and intended.

However, there's a question: does the planning phase in Standard mode do something necessary that Sequential mode skips, causing a functional regression? The planning phase runs stages 1-4 (Ideate, Research, Diagnose, Plan) which produce the manifest and test command. In Sequential mode, the full driver run includes stages 1-4 anyway, so the manifest/test command are still produced. So no regression.

One more: In Standard mode, planning runs in parallel WORKTREES (one per task), so multiple tasks' planning happens concurrently. In Sequential mode, each task runs entirely (1-11) before the next starts. This is exactly "one at a time". Correct.

Now let me verify the tests are valid. The tests are structural-validation tests that read source files and assert string presence. They don't test runtime behavior of the Sequential mode. That's a limitation but consistent with the test plan (stage 5 chose structural validation). The stage-6 added 6 new tests. Let me verify the test count and that they'd pass.

Let me check a few specific test assertions that could be wrong:
1. `RunAllMode_HasStandardAndSequentialValues` — asserts 2 values. Enum has Standard, Sequential. ✓
2. `DrainAsync_AcceptsRunAllModeParameter` — reflects on RelayQueueController.DrainAsync, finds param named "mode" of type RunAllMode, IsOptional. The signature is `DrainAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken = default, RunAllMode mode = RunAllMode.Standard)`. ✓ Both optional.
3. `DrainQueue_PassesRunAllModeToController` — reads Execution.cs, asserts contains "SelectedRunAllMode" and "DrainAsync(mode:". The call is `controller.DrainAsync(mode: SelectedRunAllMode)`. ✓

Now the param ORDER. The original DrainAsync was `DrainAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)`. The new one is `DrainAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken = default, RunAllMode mode = RunAllMode.Standard)`. The caller uses named arg `mode:`, so order doesn't matter. ✓

Let me check if there are OTHER callers of DrainAsync that might break. Let me search.

All other callers of DrainAsync use either `DrainAsync()` (no args, defaults apply → Standard mode) or `DrainAsync(cts.Token)` (positional CancellationToken). Since the new signature is `DrainAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken = default, RunAllMode mode = RunAllMode.Standard)`, the positional CancellationToken call `DrainAsync(cts.Token)` still maps to the first param correctly. So no caller breaks. Good — backward compatible.

Now let me verify the test file line counts against the 300-line guard, and check the RunAllMode.cs is under the Queue namespace correctly referenced. Also I should double check: does the test project reference VisualRelay.Core so it can see RunAllMode and RelayQueueController? The tests use `using VisualRelay.Core.Queue;` and `using VisualRelay.Core.Execution;` and `using VisualRelay.Cli;`. Let me verify the test project references these. Actually the existing tests already reference RelayQueueController and RelayStages, so the references exist. RunAllMode is in VisualRelay.Core/Queue which is already referenced.

Line counts:
- RunAllModesTests.cs: 277 lines (shown 1-277). Under 300. ✓
- RunAllModesTests.CommandDispatch.cs: 132 lines. ✓
- RunAllModesTests.StageContracts.cs: 249 lines. ✓
- RunAllMode.cs: 15 lines. ✓

Now, the key remaining question: are there any tests that would FAIL due to the new structural assertions being wrong about the codebase? Let me verify a few that I haven't confirmed against actual source:

1. The StageContracts test `OnlyIdeateAndCommitStages_HaveNoneFilesScope` asserts stage 1 AND 11 have Files=="none". Let me verify RelayStages has stage 11 (Commit) with files="none". The RunAllModesTests.cs line 159 shows `[InlineData(11, "Commit", "cheap", "none")]` and the metadata test checks this. So stage 11 files="none". But wait — the Stage 3 Diagnose excerpt said "[RelayStages.cs line 9] Stage(1, 'Ideate', 'cheap', 'none', ...)". And the plan said "OnlyIdeateStage_HasNoneFilesScope" (only stage 1). But the actual implemented test says BOTH 1 and 11 have none. I need to verify stage 11's actual files scope in RelayStages.cs. If stage 11 is "none" the test passes; if it's "driver" or something else, the test fails.

Actually the AllRelayStages_HaveCorrectMetadata theory at line 159 asserts stage 11 = ("Commit","cheap","none"). And StageContracts AllStages_HaveValidFilesScope allows {none,some,all,driver}. And OnlyIdeateAndCommitStages_HaveNoneFilesScope asserts 1 and 11 are "none", others not "none". These must be consistent with actual RelayStages.cs. The stage-5 author said these pass (0 failures). Let me verify RelayStages.cs to be sure since this is the crux.

2. The CommitStage_HasEmptySystemPrompt asserts commit.SystemPrompt == string.Empty (not just whitespace). And AllLlmStageContracts_ReferenceFencedJsonBlock iterates stages where Kind=="llm". Need Commit Kind=="driver". Let me verify RelayStages.cs.

Let me read RelayStages.cs.

Now I have the authoritative RelayStages. Let me verify the test assertions against it:

Stage 11: `new(11, "Commit", "cheap", "driver", "none", "git", string.Empty, string.Empty)`. 
The RelayStageDefinition constructor signature — from the `Stage` helper: `new(number, name, tier, "llm", files, commands, SystemPromptFor(name), contract)`. So the constructor order is (number, name, tier, kind, files, commands, systemPrompt, outputContract). 

For stage 11: `new(11, "Commit", "cheap", "driver", "none", "git", string.Empty, string.Empty)`:
- number=11, name="Commit", tier="cheap", kind="driver", files="none", commands="git", systemPrompt=string.Empty, outputContract=string.Empty.

Wait! The stage 11 tuple has 8 args: (11, "Commit", "cheap", "driver", "none", "git", string.Empty, string.Empty). Mapping to (number, name, tier, kind, files, commands, systemPrompt, outputContract):
- kind = "driver" ✓
- files = "none" ✓ (the 5th positional after kind)

Hold on. Let me re-map. The `Stage` helper calls `new(number, name, tier, "llm", files, commands, ...)`. So positional order is: number, name, tier, kind, files, commands, systemPrompt, outputContract.

Stage 11: `new(11, "Commit", "cheap", "driver", "none", "git", string.Empty, string.Empty)`:

2. name = "Commit"
3. tier = "cheap"
4. kind = "driver"

So stage.Files == "none" for stage 11. ✓

Now check tests:
- `AllRelayStages_HaveCorrectMetadata(11, "Commit", "cheap", "none")`: asserts name="Commit", tier="cheap", files="none", SystemPrompt not null, OutputContract not null, and since number==11 skips the NotEmpty check. ✓ PASS.

Now, the decisive technical point about Avalonia compiled bindings and static members:

Avalonia's compiled binding source generator resolves the member path against the `x:DataType`. When the member is a static property, the generated accessor C# code accesses it statically. I'm now confident about this specific Avalonia behavior: compiled bindings in Avalonia 11 DO support static properties — the generated `GetValue` calls `MainWindowViewModel.RunAllModeOptions` directly. The reason: compiled bindings are not runtime reflection; they're source-generated typed accessors. For a static member, the generator emits static access. Since this app uses compiled bindings by default and x:DataType is set, the binding is compiled → resolves the static → populates the ComboBox.

Let me also sanity-check the Sequential behavior claim once more against the code path for a SINGLE queued task vs multiple. In Sequential mode, Phase 1 skipped. Phase 2 `while (queue.Count > 0)` runs each task via `_runner.RunTaskAsync` (GuiTaskRunner → RelayDriver with Resume:true). For a fresh task, firstStageToRun=1 → runs stages 1-11 fully, awaits completion, THEN pops next task. So tasks are strictly serial. Even the planning stages (1-4) run serially per-task, not in parallel across tasks. This precisely matches "make sure each task is run in sequence, one at a time, rather than making the first few stages in parallel." Correct.

Edge: In Standard mode, Phase 1 parallelizes stages 1-4 across tasks in separate worktrees, then Phase 2 serially runs stages 5-11 per task. The user's description "making the first few stages in parallel like they are today" matches Standard's Phase 1. Sequential removes that. Correct mapping.
